name: Acambaro Figures
description: The Acambaro figures are a collection of thousands of ceramic artifacts discovered near Acambaro, Guanajuato, Mexico, between 1944 and 1951. The primary proponent, Waldemar Julsrud, claimed the figures represented a civilization contemporary with or predating the dinosaurs, suggesting human-dinosaur co-existence and challenging established timelines of human evolution and the extinction of dinosaurs. The collection includes depictions of various animals, including alleged dinosaurs, as well as human figures and artifacts resembling tools and weapons. Proponents argue the sheer number and variety of the figures, along with the presence of apparent dinosaur depictions, support their claims. Mainstream archaeology and paleontology overwhelmingly reject the authenticity of the Acambaro figures. Scientific analysis, including thermoluminescence dating, has indicated that the figures were created relatively recently, likely around the time of their discovery. The consensus view is that the figures are a hoax, possibly created by local farmers using readily available clay and influenced by the growing popularity of dinosaur depictions in popular culture. The lack of archaeological context and the absence of corroborating evidence from other sites further undermine the claims of authenticity. The controversy surrounding the Acambaro figures highlights the tension between alternative interpretations of history and established scientific methodologies. The figures continue to be cited by some as evidence against mainstream views on human and dinosaur history.
subtitle: Ceramic figures: Dinosaurs and ancient humans?

claims.claim: The figures depict dinosaurs and humans coexisting.
claims.evidence: Figures resembling dinosaurs alongside human figures.
claims.criticism: Figures were made recently, based on dating and style analysis. Depictions are inaccurate and reflect popular culture, not scientific knowledge.

claims.claim: The figures represent a previously unknown ancient civilization.
claims.evidence: Large number of figures with diverse styles and depictions.
claims.criticism: No other archaeological evidence supports the existence of such a civilization in the Acambaro region. Figures lack proper archaeological context.

claims.claim: The figures prove that dinosaurs did not go extinct millions of years ago.
claims.evidence: Depictions of dinosaurs suggest they lived much more recently.
claims.criticism: Fossil evidence and geological dating overwhelmingly support the extinction of non-avian dinosaurs 66 million years ago. The figures are dated to the mid-20th century.

keyFigures.name: Waldemar Julsrud
keyFigures.role: Collector and primary proponent of the figures' authenticity.

keyFigures.name: Charles DiPeso
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who investigated the site and concluded the figures were a hoax.

mythologyConnections: Possible inspiration from indigenous creation myths involving animal-human hybrids
Potential link to legends of giants or ancient races

timeline.event: Waldemar Julsrud discovers and collects the Acambaro figures.
Initial investigations raise doubts about the figures' authenticity.
Charles DiPeso conducts an official archaeological investigation and concludes the figures are a hoax.
The Acambaro figures continue to be debated and promoted by some as evidence against mainstream science.
